qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Cluster proxy SSRF lets authenticated users reach loopback and private HTTP services
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding is an authenticated SSRF in cluster node registration and reverse-proxy routing (`POST /api/nodes`, `X-Node-ID`, and `internal/middleware/proxy.go`), while the advisory is Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking caused by `websocket.Upgrader.CheckOrigin` always returning true on WebSocket endpoints plus cookie-based browser authentication.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: The session produced one accepted qualifying finding, but it targets a different root cause. The reported issue is an authenticated SSRF through attacker-controlled cluster node URLs and proxy middleware, whereas the advisory describes CSWSH on WebSocket endpoints caused by unconditional `CheckOrigin` acceptance and cookie-based ambient authentication.
evidence: The advisory describes `websocket.Upgrader{CheckOrigin: func(*http.Request) bool { return true }}` across WebSocket handlers such as `api/terminal/pty.go`, `api/event/websocket.go`, `api/nginx/websocket.go`, and related files, and it relies on browser cookies being accepted for authentication.

The accepted finding report instead identifies `POST /api/nodes`, attacker-controlled `X-Node-ID`, `api/cluster/node.go:51`, and `internal/middleware/proxy.go:33`, where `httputil.NewSingleHostReverseProxy(baseUrl)` forwards requests to an attacker-chosen `node.URL`.

The finding's demonstrated exploit is a low-privilege authenticated user retrieving `INTERNAL-ONLY-SECRET` from a loopback-only HTTP service; it does not involve a browser victim, cross-origin WebSocket upgrade, `Origin` validation, or cookie-driven CSWSH.
Repository overlap alone is insufficient: the finding and the advisory do not share the same vulnerable function, sink, guard failure, or source-to-sink path.
